title: Plaited rush mat
subtitle: A woven rush placemat or trivet in the traditional plaiting method
excerpt: A plaited rush mat in the checkerboard over-under weave: 45 cm long and 30 cm wide, using soaked bulrush. Plaiting is the simplest form of rush work; no frame, no bodkin work, just warp strands laid down and weft strands woven through. The finished mat is dense enough to act as a trivet for pots and doubles as a placemat.
sourceNotes: Traditional British rush plaiting for domestic matwork; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for plaited rush mat dimensions and border finishing.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried common bulrush or soft rush, soaked before use. The traditional British material for plaited and coiled matwork.
Over-under interlacing of flat strips or strands; the simplest form of basketry weave, producing a dense, regular checkerboard pattern.
The fixed strands that are laid down first in plaiting work; the weft strands are woven through them.

content.text: Rush plaiting; the over-under interlacing of flat
rush
strands; is the simplest form of basketry weave. There is no coiling, no stake insertion, no complicated border: just
plaiting
one strand over and under the next in sequence. The result is a dense mat that works as a trivet, a placemat, or a base for a small coiled basket.
The rush

Use dried bulrush or soft rush soaked for 10 minutes in cold water and left under a damp cloth for 30 minutes before work. Select strands of even width; ideally 8 to 10 mm flat width when pressed. Wider strands produce a bolder checkerboard; narrower strands produce a finer weave but require more strands to cover the same area. You will need approximately 80 strands of 55 cm length for a 45 by 30 cm mat.
Method
Lay the
warp
strands first, then weave the weft strands through them at 90 degrees. Work on a flat board or table; the mat will try to curl at the edges if not pressed flat at each stage.

Lay 30 warp strands side by side horizontally on the work surface, each 45 cm long. Secure the ends temporarily under a weight or tape them to the board. The strands should lie flat and parallel, touching but not overlapping.

Weave the first weft strand vertically through the warp: over strand 1, under strand 2, over strand 3, and so on across the full width. Push the weft strand to the top of the warp and press it flat. Weave the second weft strand in the opposite sequence: under strand 1, over strand 2, under strand 3. Push it up snugly against the first.

Continue alternating weft strands; over-under then under-over; until the mat reaches 30 cm in the weft direction. Each weft strand should lock the previous one in place by pressing it firmly down before starting the next. Use the bodkin to open the weave if a strand is difficult to pass through.

Finish the border: fold the protruding end of each warp strand back over the final weft strand and tuck it under the nearest parallel strand using the bodkin to open the passage. Repeat for each warp strand end, alternating the tuck direction on each one to lock the border firmly.
Finish the weft ends the same way: fold each protruding weft end back and tuck it under the nearest strand perpendicular to it. Trim any remaining ends close with the sloyd knife.
Finishing
Press the finished mat flat under a board for 24 hours while it dries. The rush will lighten as it dries and the weave will tighten. A well-woven mat lies completely flat without weighting.
Variations
Stripe: introduce one or two strands of dyed rush in the weft at regular intervals for a stripe pattern across the mat.
Small trivet: reduce to 20 by 20 cm; useful under hot pots or as a coaster for large mugs.
Twill variation: skip two strands instead of one (over 2, under 2) for a diagonal pattern across the mat surface. Requires even numbers of warp and weft strands.
Care
Wipe down with a dry cloth. Rush mats kept consistently damp will mould; store dry. A brief misting with water if the mat becomes very dry and brittle will restore some flexibility.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the plaited rush mat.

items.symptom: Mat edges curl upward after drying
items.cause: Border tucks not pulled tight, or mat not pressed flat during drying
items.fix: Dampen the mat slightly and press under a weighted board for 48 hours. In future, pull each border tuck as tight as possible before the mat dries.

items.symptom: Weave loose in the centre but tight at the edges
items.cause: Weft strands pushed up inconsistently; more firmly at the edges than the centre
items.fix: After inserting each weft strand, press across the full width with both hands before starting the next one. The strand should lie uniformly snug against the previous one from edge to edge.

items.symptom: Rush snaps on the border fold
items.cause: Rush under-soaked or left too long after soaking before working
items.fix: The border fold is the sharpest bend in the whole project; keep the rush well moistened. Re-dampen any strand that has dried before attempting the border fold.
